name: Counters and Numbers
summary: Use the two Korean number systems with the right counter words to count people, things, and time.
explanation:
Korean has two number systems. Native Korean numbers (하나, 둘, 셋...) count objects, people, age, and hours. Sino-Korean numbers (일, 이, 삼...) count money, minutes, dates, phone numbers, and most larger amounts. Choosing the right system is a core early skill.

To count things, you need a counter (단위 명사) after the number. 명 counts people, 개 counts general objects, 마리 counts animals, 잔 counts cups, and 시 counts the hour. The pattern is noun + number + counter: 사과 세 개 (three apples), 사람 두 명 (two people).

Several native numbers change shape before a counter: 하나 becomes 한, 둘 becomes 두, 셋 becomes 세, 넷 becomes 네, and 스물 becomes 스무. So 'one cup of coffee' is 커피 한 잔, not 커피 하나 잔. Telling time famously mixes both systems: 세 시 삼십 분 (3:30) uses native 세 for the hour and Sino-Korean 삼십 for the minutes.

examples.native: 사과 세 개 주세요. (sagwa se gae juseyo.)
사람 두 명이 왔어요. (saram du myeongi wasseoyo.)
커피 한 잔 주세요. (keopi han jan juseyo.)
고양이 네 마리가 있어요. (goyangi ne mariga isseoyo.)

commonMistakes: Using Sino-Korean numbers to count objects. Say 사과 세 개, not 사과 삼 개.
Forgetting that 하나, 둘, 셋, 넷 shorten to 한, 두, 세, 네 before a counter.
Dropping the counter entirely. Korean needs 개, 명, 마리, 잔 and so on after the number.
Using native numbers for minutes or money. Minutes and money take Sino-Korean: 삼십 분, 천 원.
Mixing up 명 (people) and 개 (objects). People are counted with 명, never 개.
tip: Native numbers (한, 두, 세) plus counters for things and hours; Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼) for minutes, money, and dates.
